What kind of data was the system trained.question_interpretation: This asks what type of information the AI system uses to make its predictions — what was collected, from whom, and in what form, so clinicians can judge whether the data is relevant to their patient population.
What kind of data was the system trained.answer: The system was trained on survey-based data collected from a nationally representative telephone survey of households in the United States. The data includes demographic information (age group, sex, ethnicity, education level, income, marital status, employment status), behavioural factors (e.g. hand washing, face mask use, avoidance of large gatherings), clinical indicators (e.g. whether a doctor recommended the H1N1 or seasonal vaccine, presence of chronic medical conditions, healthcare worker status, health insurance status), and opinion-based responses (e.g. perceived vaccine effectiveness, perceived risk of illness, worry about side effects from vaccination). All features are either categorical or ordinal in nature, captured as self-reported responses from survey participants aged 6 months or older.

What is the source of the training data?.question_interpretation: This asks where the data originated — which institution collected it, under what authority, and using what method — so that clinicians and public health professionals can assess its credibility and relevance.
What is the source of the training data?.answer: The training data comes from the National 2009 H1N1 Flu Survey (NHFS), which was sponsored by the National Center for Immunization and Respiratory Diseases (NCIRD) and conducted jointly by NCIRD and the National Center for Health Statistics (NCHS), both part of the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC). It was a list-assisted random-digit-dialling telephone survey of households, conducted between October 2009 and June 2010, designed to monitor influenza immunisation coverage during the 2009-10 season. The dataset was made available through DrivenData's public competition 'Flu Shot Learning: Predict H1N1 and Seasonal Flu Vaccines'.

How were the labels/ground-truth produce.question_interpretation: This asks how we know whether someone actually received a vaccine — i.e. is the 'truth' the model learns from based on verified clinical records or self-reported information?
How were the labels/ground-truth produce.answer: The labels (ground truth) are based on self-reported survey responses. Each respondent was asked whether they received the H1N1 flu vaccine and whether they received the seasonal flu vaccine, recorded as binary values (0 = No, 1 = Yes). These labels were not verified against clinical records, immunisation registries, or laboratory confirmation. This is an important consideration: self-reported vaccination status can be subject to recall bias and social desirability bias, which may affect the reliability of the labels the AI system has learnt from.

What is the sample size of the training.question_interpretation: This asks how many individuals' data was used to train the system, which helps clinicians understand how robust the model's learnt patterns are likely to be.
What is the sample size of the training.answer: The training dataset contains 26,707 survey respondents with 35 input features and 2 target variables (H1N1 vaccine uptake and seasonal vaccine uptake). Of these, approximately 21% reported receiving the H1N1 vaccine and approximately 47% reported receiving the seasonal flu vaccine. For the model explanation and what-if analysis components, a subset of 5,342 observations was used. The overall sample size is reasonably large for a survey-based study, though the class imbalance — particularly for H1N1 vaccination — is a factor to consider when interpreting the system's performance.

What dataset(s) is the system NOT using?.question_interpretation: This asks what kinds of clinically relevant data are absent from the system, helping clinicians understand what the AI cannot account for in its predictions.
What dataset(s) is the system NOT using?.answer: The system does not use any clinical or laboratory data such as electronic health records, immunisation registries, serological test results, or pharmacy dispensing records. It does not include clinical details about specific chronic conditions (only a binary indicator of whether any chronic condition is present). It does not capture data on prior adverse reactions to vaccines, patient-provider relationship quality, access to vaccination sites, or real-time public health messaging exposure. Geographic data is anonymised to broad regional codes rather than specific locations. The system also does not incorporate data from outside the United States or from time periods other than the 2009-10 flu season.

What are the potential limitations/biase.question_interpretation: This asks what systematic issues in the data could lead the AI to produce skewed, unfair, or unreliable predictions for certain groups or situations.
What are the potential limitations/biase.answer: Several potential biases and limitations exist. First, the data is entirely self-reported, introducing recall bias and social desirability bias in both the features and the target labels. Second, there is substantial missing data: health insurance status is missing for nearly 46% of respondents, employment industry and occupation for approximately 50%, income level for about 17%, and doctor recommendation fields for around 8%. Third, the data reflects a single flu season (2009-10) in the United States during the H1N1 pandemic, which may not generalise to other seasons, populations, or countries. Fourth, there is a notable class imbalance in H1N1 vaccine uptake (only 21% vaccinated), which can affect the system's ability to correctly identify vaccinated individuals. Fifth, demographic variables such as ethnicity and geographic region are encoded with anonymised labels, limiting interpretability. The most common age group is 65+ years and the most common ethnicity is White, meaning the model may perform differently for underrepresented demographic subgroups.

What kind of output does the system give.question_interpretation: This asks what form the AI system's prediction takes — whether it gives a simple yes/no answer, a probability score, or something else — so clinicians know how to read and interpret it.
What kind of output does the system give.answer: The system outputs a predicted probability score between 0 and 1, representing the estimated likelihood that a given individual received the H1N1 flu vaccine. This probability is then converted into a binary prediction (vaccinated or not vaccinated) using a decision threshold of 0.5. For example, a prediction of 0.701 means the system estimates a roughly 70% likelihood that the individual received the H1N1 vaccine.

What does the system output mean?.question_interpretation: This asks how a clinician or public health professional should interpret the numerical output — what does a high or low score actually signify in practical terms?
What does the system output mean?.answer: A higher predicted probability (closer to 1.0) indicates the system believes the individual is more likely to have received the H1N1 vaccine, based on their survey responses about demographics, behaviours, opinions, and clinical factors. A lower probability (closer to 0.0) suggests the individual is less likely to have been vaccinated. When the probability exceeds 0.5, the system classifies the individual as 'vaccinated'; below 0.5, as 'not vaccinated'. It is important to note that this is a retrospective prediction of self-reported behaviour, not a clinical diagnosis or a recommendation about future vaccination.

What is the scope of the system's capabi.question_interpretation: This asks what the system can and cannot do — its intended use and boundaries — so that clinicians do not apply it beyond its designed purpose.
What is the scope of the system's capabi.answer: The system is designed to predict the likelihood that an individual received the H1N1 flu vaccine during the 2009-10 season, based on survey-derived features. It can identify patterns associated with vaccine uptake at a population level, such as the influence of doctor recommendations or opinions about vaccine effectiveness. It cannot diagnose illness, recommend treatment, predict future vaccine uptake in different contexts, or account for factors not present in the survey data. It is not designed for individual clinical decision-making but may support public health research and understanding of vaccination behaviour drivers.

How is the output used for other system.question_interpretation: This asks whether the AI's prediction feeds into any other automated system, pipeline, or decision-support tool.
How is the output used for other system.answer: The predicted probabilities are used downstream for explainability and what-if analysis components. The probability scores feed into an explanation module that uses techniques such as variable importance analysis, partial dependence profiles, and individual-level break-down plots to explain why the system made a particular prediction. They also feed into a what-if dashboard that uses ceteris paribus profiles to explore how changes in individual features would affect the predicted probability. There is no indication that the output is connected to any clinical decision-support system or automated workflow beyond this analytical framework.

How should I best utilize the output of.question_interpretation: This asks for practical guidance on how a healthcare professional should use the system's output in a meaningful and safe way.
How should I best utilize the output of.answer: The output is best used as a research and public health planning tool rather than for individual patient decisions. Healthcare professionals can use it to understand which factors — such as doctor recommendations, perceived vaccine effectiveness, or health worker status — are most strongly associated with vaccine uptake in a population. The explainability features allow users to interrogate why certain predictions are made, and the what-if analysis enables exploration of hypothetical scenarios (e.g. 'What would happen to predicted uptake if more individuals received a doctor's recommendation?'). Any insights should be considered alongside current clinical evidence and local public health context.

How should the output fit in my workflow.question_interpretation: This asks where in a clinician's or public health professional's workflow this system's output is most appropriately placed.
How should the output fit in my workflow.answer: This system is best positioned as a retrospective analytical tool within public health surveillance or research workflows. It could support post-season reviews of vaccination campaigns, help identify subpopulations with lower predicted uptake for targeted outreach, or inform the design of future vaccination programmes. It should not replace clinical judgement, patient interaction, or established immunisation guidelines. The what-if analysis component is particularly suited to scenario planning and policy development discussions.

What is the source of the information ob.question_interpretation: This asks where the data and predictions originate from, ensuring transparency about the provenance of the information being presented.
What is the source of the information ob.answer: All information objects — input features, labels, and derived predictions — originate from the National 2009 H1N1 Flu Survey (NHFS) conducted by the CDC. The survey data was collected via random-digit-dialling telephone interviews of US households between October 2009 and June 2010. The predictions are generated by a machine learning model trained on this survey data, and the explanations are produced using the DALEX explainability framework applied to the trained model.

What is the scope of the output data?.question_interpretation: This asks what range of predictions the system can produce and for which population its outputs are valid.
What is the scope of the output data?.answer: The output data covers predictions of H1N1 vaccine uptake for individuals aged 6 months or older residing in the United States during the 2009-10 flu season. Predictions are bounded between 0 and 1 (probability) and are valid only for individuals whose characteristics fall within the range of the training data. The system should not be assumed to generalise to other countries, other vaccine types beyond H1N1, other time periods, or populations with substantially different demographic or healthcare access profiles.

What is the amount of the output data?.question_interpretation: This asks how many predictions or outputs the system generates and in what volume, so users understand the scale of the output.
What is the amount of the output data?.answer: The system generates one predicted probability per individual respondent. For the full training dataset, this amounts to 26,707 predictions. For the explainability and what-if analysis components, outputs are generated for a subset of 5,342 observations. Each prediction is accompanied by explanatory outputs including feature-level contributions (break-down values) and what-if scenario results, which provide additional context for each individual prediction.

What logic is used for the initial outpu.question_interpretation: This asks what underlying reasoning or algorithm produces the system's first prediction before any post-processing or explanation is applied.
What logic is used for the initial outpu.answer: The initial prediction is produced by a tuned XGBoost (Extreme Gradient Boosting) classifier. This algorithm works by building a sequence of decision trees, where each new tree attempts to correct the errors of the previous ones. The model takes in all available survey features — demographics, behaviours, opinions, and clinical indicators — processes them through a preprocessing pipeline, and outputs a probability score. The probability is derived from the combined output of 200 sequential decision trees, each with a maximum depth of 4, using a learning rate of 0.1. The binary classification is then determined by applying a threshold of 0.5 to this probability.

How accurate/precise/reliable are the pr.question_interpretation: This asks how well the system's predictions match reality — how often it gets it right and how confident clinicians can be in its outputs.
How accurate/precise/reliable are the pr.answer: The system achieved a best ROC AUC score of 0.837 during cross-validated hyperparameter tuning, indicating good discriminative ability — that is, the system is reasonably effective at distinguishing between individuals who did and did not receive the H1N1 vaccine. The ROC AUC of 0.837 means that if you took one vaccinated and one unvaccinated individual at random, the system would correctly assign a higher probability to the vaccinated individual approximately 84% of the time. This was achieved using 5-fold cross-validation across 50 candidate parameter configurations, providing a robust estimate of performance. However, detailed precision, recall, and F1 scores by class are not fully specified in the available documentation, which limits a complete assessment of reliability for specific use cases.

How often does the system make mistakes?.question_interpretation: This asks about the system's error rate — how frequently it misclassifies individuals — so clinicians can gauge the risk of acting on incorrect predictions.
How often does the system make mistakes?.answer: Based on the available performance data, the system has an ROC AUC of 0.837, meaning it has meaningful but imperfect discriminative ability. Given the class imbalance (only 21% of respondents received the H1N1 vaccine), the system is likely to make more errors when predicting the minority class (vaccinated individuals). Specific false positive and false negative rates are not fully detailed in the available documentation. Clinicians and public health professionals should be aware that the system will occasionally misclassify vaccinated individuals as unvaccinated (and vice versa), and the frequency of such errors may vary across demographic subgroups.

In what situations is the system likely.question_interpretation: This asks under what conditions or for which types of individuals the system performs well or poorly, helping clinicians identify when to trust or question its output.
In what situations is the system likely.answer: The system is likely to be most accurate for individuals whose characteristics are well-represented in the training data — particularly older adults (65+ years), White respondents, college graduates, and those with clear signals such as a doctor's recommendation or strong opinions about vaccine effectiveness. The system may be less reliable for underrepresented groups, individuals with substantial missing data (e.g. those without health insurance information, which is missing for nearly 46% of the dataset), or individuals whose vaccination decision was driven by factors not captured in the survey (e.g. access barriers, culturally specific concerns). The class imbalance in H1N1 vaccination (21% uptake) also means the system may be less reliable at correctly identifying vaccinated individuals.

What are the limitations of the system?.question_interpretation: This asks about the broader constraints and weaknesses of the AI system that users should be aware of before relying on its outputs.
What are the limitations of the system?.answer: Key limitations include: (1) the system is trained exclusively on US data from a single flu season (2009-10) during a pandemic, limiting generalisability to other settings or time periods; (2) ground-truth labels are self-reported and not clinically verified; (3) substantial missing data exists for several important features, particularly health insurance (46% missing) and employment-related fields (~50% missing); (4) the system predicts retrospective behaviour rather than future vaccination intent; (5) anonymised encoding of geographic and employment variables limits clinical interpretability; (6) the class imbalance in H1N1 vaccine uptake may bias predictions towards the majority (unvaccinated) class; and (7) the model does not account for clinical detail beyond binary indicators of chronic conditions and healthcare worker status.

What kind of mistakes is the system like.question_interpretation: This asks about the specific types of errors the system tends to make — whether it is more likely to miss true cases or falsely flag non-cases — which is critical for understanding patient safety implications.
What kind of mistakes is the system like.answer: Given the class imbalance (79% unvaccinated vs. 21% vaccinated for H1N1), the system is more likely to incorrectly predict that a vaccinated individual was not vaccinated (i.e. miss a true case of vaccination) than to falsely predict vaccination where none occurred. In public health terms, this means the system may underestimate vaccine uptake in certain subgroups. Additionally, for individuals with missing data on key predictive features (such as doctor recommendation or health insurance status), the system's predictions may be less reliable and could default towards the majority class prediction.

How does the system make predictions?.question_interpretation: This asks about the overall mechanism the AI uses to arrive at a prediction — how it processes input data and generates an output.
How does the system make predictions?.answer: The system uses an XGBoost (Extreme Gradient Boosting) classifier, which is an ensemble learning method that builds predictions by combining the outputs of many small decision trees trained sequentially. Each tree learns from the errors of the previous ones, gradually refining the prediction. The model takes a respondent's survey data — including their demographics, health behaviours, opinions about vaccines, and clinical indicators — passes it through a preprocessing pipeline (which handles missing values and encodes categorical variables), and produces a probability score estimating the likelihood that the individual received the H1N1 vaccine.

What features does the system consider?.question_interpretation: This asks which specific pieces of information the system takes into account when making a prediction, so clinicians can assess whether the inputs are clinically meaningful.
What features does the system consider?.answer: The system considers approximately 31 input features after preprocessing, grouped into several categories: (1) Opinion-related features — perceived effectiveness of H1N1 and seasonal vaccines, perceived risk of illness, and worry about vaccine side effects; (2) Clinical and healthcare features — whether a doctor recommended the H1N1 or seasonal vaccine, presence of chronic medical conditions, healthcare worker status, health insurance status, and contact with children under 6 months; (3) Behavioural features — use of antiviral medications, avoidance of close contact, face mask use, hand hygiene, avoidance of large gatherings, reduced contact outside the home, and avoidance of touching the face; (4) Demographic and socioeconomic features — age group, education, ethnicity, sex, income relative to poverty level, marital status, housing tenure, employment status, geographic region, and metropolitan area classification; and (5) Household features — number of adults and children in the household.

What is the system's overall logic?.question_interpretation: This asks for a high-level summary of the system's decision-making approach, explained in terms that are meaningful for clinical reasoning.
What is the system's overall logic?.answer: At a high level, the system's logic mirrors a form of probabilistic risk assessment. It considers multiple factors simultaneously — much as a clinician might weigh a patient's demographics, health beliefs, clinical history, and access to care when assessing vaccine uptake likelihood. The system assigns the greatest weight to factors that most strongly distinguish vaccinated from unvaccinated individuals, particularly opinions about vaccine effectiveness, doctor recommendations, and perceived risk. It combines these factors through an ensemble of decision trees that collectively capture complex interactions between features, producing a nuanced probability estimate rather than relying on any single rule or threshold.

How does it weigh different features?.question_interpretation: This asks which input factors have the most influence on the system's predictions and how their relative importance is determined.
How does it weigh different features?.answer: The system's feature weighting is determined through permutation-based variable importance analysis. The most influential features are those related to opinions about H1N1 vaccine effectiveness, doctor recommendations for the H1N1 vaccine, and perceived risk of H1N1 illness. Features are also analysed in groups: opinion-related features (perceived vaccine effectiveness and risk) and clinical features (doctor recommendations, chronic conditions) contribute most to the model's predictive accuracy. Behavioural features (hand washing, mask use, social distancing) and demographic features (age, sex, ethnicity) have comparatively less individual influence but still contribute to the overall prediction. Features such as housing tenure, geographic region, and employment status have the least individual impact.

What kind of rules does it follow?.question_interpretation: This asks whether the system follows explicit, interpretable rules — like clinical guidelines — or uses a more complex, implicit decision-making process.
What kind of rules does it follow?.answer: The system does not follow explicit, pre-defined rules like clinical guidelines. Instead, it learns implicit patterns from the training data through an ensemble of decision trees. Each tree makes a series of binary splits (e.g. 'Is the respondent's opinion of vaccine effectiveness above a certain level?') and the combined output of all 200 trees produces the final prediction. While individual trees can be interpreted as simple if-then rules, the ensemble as a whole operates as a complex, non-linear model. The explainability tools (break-down plots and partial dependence profiles) are provided to make these implicit patterns more transparent and interpretable for users.

What are the top rules/features that det.question_interpretation: This asks which specific factors are the strongest drivers of the system's predictions, so clinicians can understand what matters most to the model.
What are the top rules/features that det.answer: Based on the permutation variable importance analysis, the top features driving the system's predictions are: (1) opinion about H1N1 vaccine effectiveness — respondents who believe the vaccine is effective are substantially more likely to be predicted as vaccinated; (2) doctor recommendation for H1N1 vaccine — receiving a doctor's recommendation is a strong positive predictor; (3) opinion about H1N1 risk — those who perceive higher personal risk are more likely to be predicted as vaccinated; (4) opinion about getting sick from the H1N1 vaccine — concern about side effects is associated with lower predicted uptake; and (5) doctor recommendation for seasonal vaccine and health worker status also contribute meaningfully. These findings align with established public health evidence that provider recommendations and perceived vaccine effectiveness are among the strongest drivers of vaccination behaviour.

What kind of algorithm is used?.question_interpretation: This asks for the specific type of machine learning algorithm powering the system, so technically minded clinicians or oversight bodies can evaluate its suitability.
What kind of algorithm is used?.answer: The system uses XGBoost (Extreme Gradient Boosting), a gradient-boosted decision tree algorithm. Three algorithms were evaluated during development — Logistic Regression, Random Forest, and XGBoost — and the tuned XGBoost model was selected as the best-performing option based on ROC AUC score. XGBoost is widely used in healthcare-related prediction tasks due to its strong performance with tabular data, ability to handle missing values, and built-in regularisation to reduce overfitting. The model includes a preprocessing pipeline that handles categorical variable encoding and data preparation before the XGBoost classifier generates predictions.

How were the parameters set?.question_interpretation: This asks how the model's internal settings (hyperparameters) were chosen — whether through expert judgement, automated search, or some other method.
How were the parameters set?.answer: The model's hyperparameters were set using randomised search with 5-fold cross-validation (RandomizedSearchCV), evaluating 50 candidate parameter combinations (totalling 250 model fits). The best configuration identified was: 200 decision trees (n_estimators), maximum tree depth of 4, learning rate of 0.1, subsample ratio of 0.8, column sample ratio of 0.8, minimum child weight of 3, L1 regularisation (alpha) of 0.1, L2 regularisation (lambda) of 100, gamma of 0, and scale_pos_weight of 1. This systematic tuning process helps ensure the model balances predictive accuracy with generalisation, reducing the risk of overfitting to the training data.

How have updates affected the system?.question_interpretation: This asks whether the system has been updated since its initial development, and if so, how those changes have affected its behaviour or performance.
How have updates affected the system?.answer: The available documentation does not describe any updates, versioning history, or iterative changes to the system after its initial development. The system appears to be a single-version analytical model built for research and educational purposes. Without documented update logs, it is not possible to assess whether changes have been made or how they may have affected performance. Any future deployment should include a clear versioning and change management process.

Does the system learn specifically from.question_interpretation: This asks whether the system adapts or changes its behaviour based on individual user interactions — an important question for consistency and safety.
Does the system learn specifically from.answer: No. The system does not learn from individual user interactions. It is a static model trained on a fixed dataset and does not update its parameters or predictions based on new inputs, feedback, or usage patterns. Every user interacting with the system will receive predictions based on the same underlying model. The what-if analysis component allows users to explore hypothetical scenarios by modifying input features, but these explorations do not alter the model itself.

Why is the system using or not using a g.question_interpretation: This asks about the rationale behind the design choices — why certain algorithms, features, or data sources were included or excluded.
Why is the system using or not using a g.answer: The system evaluated three candidate algorithms — Logistic Regression, Random Forest, and XGBoost — and selected XGBoost based on its superior ROC AUC performance during cross-validated benchmarking. The features used are those available in the NHFS survey dataset; no additional external datasets were incorporated. Certain features with very high missingness (such as employment industry and occupation, each missing for approximately 50% of respondents) were retained in the dataset but may contribute less reliably to predictions. The respondent ID was dropped as it carries no predictive information. The seasonal vaccine target was separated to focus the model specifically on H1N1 vaccine prediction.

What are the results of other people usi.question_interpretation: This asks whether there is evidence from other users, external validation studies, or real-world deployments that can help assess the system's reliability.
What are the results of other people usi.answer: The available documentation does not provide information about external validation, third-party evaluations, or real-world deployment results. The system was developed within an academic context at the Freie Universität Berlin in collaboration with the Robert Koch Institute, and the underlying dataset originates from a public data science competition. Without published external validation studies or user feedback reports, it is not possible to confirm how the system performs in practice beyond the reported cross-validation results.

Who is responsible for this system? Who.question_interpretation: This asks about accountability — who developed and maintains the system, so that users know whom to contact with concerns or questions.
Who is responsible for this system? Who.answer: The system was developed by Henry El-Jawhari at the Freie Universität (FU) Berlin and the Robert Koch Institute (RKI). The Robert Koch Institute is Germany's federal government agency and research institute responsible for disease control and prevention. The underlying data was collected by the US Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) through the National Center for Immunization and Respiratory Diseases (NCIRD) and the National Center for Health Statistics (NCHS). The competition dataset was hosted by DrivenData.

What are the affordances of the system?.question_interpretation: This asks what interactive capabilities the system provides to users — what actions can they take and what feedback does the system offer?
What are the affordances of the system?.answer: The system provides several interactive analytical components: (1) an exploratory data analysis module that allows users to review the distribution, completeness, and characteristics of the training data; (2) a modelling module that shows how different algorithms were benchmarked and how the final model was selected and tuned; (3) an explanations module that provides global explanations (variable importance, partial dependence profiles) and local explanations (individual break-down plots and ceteris paribus profiles) to help users understand why the model made a specific prediction; and (4) a what-if dashboard that allows users to modify individual input features and observe how the predicted probability changes in response. These components are presented through interactive visualisations including plotly-based charts.

What does this UI element do?.question_interpretation: This asks for clarification on the function of specific interface components, so users can navigate and use the system effectively.
What does this UI element do?.answer: The system's interface includes several key visual elements: (1) Variable importance plots — bar charts showing which features contribute most to the model's overall predictive accuracy, ranked from most to least important; (2) Partial dependence profiles (PDP) — line charts showing how the predicted probability changes on average as a single feature varies across its range, helping users understand the direction and shape of each feature's influence; (3) Ceteris paribus profiles — similar to PDPs but applied to individual observations, showing how changing one feature for a specific person would alter their predicted probability while all other features remain fixed; (4) Break-down plots — waterfall-style charts that decompose an individual prediction into the contribution of each feature, showing how each factor pushes the prediction up or down from the baseline. These visualisations are designed to make the model's reasoning transparent and interpretable for non-technical users.
